grounding_note: Expansion batch: more professional-relationship and harm-mechanism disclosures.

scenarios.opening_query: The surgeon fixed my hernia but something has been wrong ever since and nobody will say why.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: General Litigation > Malpractice (Medical) || General Litigation > Products Liability
scenarios.hidden_fact: My new doctor showed me the recall notice — the mesh implanted in me was withdrawn from the market for exactly the complications I have.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The mesh they implanted was recalled — withdrawn for exactly the complications I'm having.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a recalled device rather than surgical technique caused the harm
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The mesh is fine — the second surgeon says the first one placed it against the standard technique and nicked a nerve.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The mesh is fine — the second surgeon says it was placed wrong and a nerve got nicked.
scenarios.vetting_note: Device-recall vs technique-error is the canonical med-mal/products fork.

scenarios.opening_query: My accountant's mistakes triggered an audit that's costing me everything.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: General Litigation > General Litigation || Taxation > Personal Income
scenarios.hidden_fact: The urgent fight is with the IRS itself — the audit closes in a month and the proposed assessment is $85,000; suing the accountant can wait.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The urgent fight is the IRS — the audit closes in a month with an $85,000 proposed assessment. The accountant can wait.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the immediate need is the tax defense or the negligence claim
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The IRS side settled — now I want the accountant held responsible for the penalties his errors caused.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The IRS part settled — now I want the accountant to answer for the penalties.
scenarios.vetting_note: Sequencing disclosure: defend the audit first vs pursue the professional after.

scenarios.opening_query: A counselor at my kids' school handled a sensitive situation so badly that my family is in crisis.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: General Litigation > General Litigation || General Litigation > Child Abuse Victims
scenarios.hidden_fact: The 'situation' is that my daughter disclosed abuse by a coach and the counselor sat on it for months — we want a case for my daughter as the victim, including against those who failed her.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: My daughter disclosed abuse by a coach and the counselor sat on it for months — we want a victim's case for her, including those who failed her.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the underlying matter is a child-abuse victim claim
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's nothing like abuse — the counselor shared my son's confidential mental-health file with his teachers and the damage is social and academic.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Not abuse — she shared my son's confidential file with his teachers and it's wrecked things for him.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 428's Child Abuse Victims boundary, reached through a realistic present-day intake.

scenarios.opening_query: I want to sue the hospital where my mother died last year. They were careless with her.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: General Litigation > Malpractice (Medical) || General Litigation > Wrongful Death
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm her only child and the personal representative — the claim is the family's loss: her death, our costs, what we've lost. That's the case I want brought.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm her personal representative — the case is the family's loss from her death itself.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the claim is the estate's death claim versus the patient's injury claim
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I should clarify — Mom survived that stay; she died months later of something unrelated. The claim is for what the negligence did to her while she lived, which her estate can still bring.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Mom actually survived that stay and died later of something unrelated — the claim is for what the negligence did to her while she lived.
scenarios.vetting_note: Death-causation fact separates wrongful death from a survival malpractice claim; subtle but legally real.

scenarios.opening_query: Our real estate agent steered us wrong on the biggest purchase of our lives.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: General Litigation > General Litigation || Real Property > General (Residential)
scenarios.hidden_fact: The core problem is the transaction itself — she never delivered the seller's disclosure showing the failed septic, and we want the sale unwound or the sellers held to it.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The heart of it is the deal — she never gave us the seller's disclosure showing the failed septic. We want the sale unwound or the sellers held to it.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether rescission of the transaction is the primary remedy
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The sellers are blameless — it's her: she represented both sides without telling us and pocketed both commissions. We want her held professionally accountable.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The sellers did nothing wrong — she represented both sides secretly and double-dipped commissions. We want her accountable.
scenarios.vetting_note: Transaction-remedy vs agent-negligence framing; the withheld-disclosure fact routes.

scenarios.opening_query: The home inspector missed things so big our 'dream house' is a money pit.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: General Litigation > General Litigation || Consumer Law > General || Real Property > General (Residential)
scenarios.hidden_fact: His contract caps liability at the $450 inspection fee — I'm told the fight is whether that consumer contract term is enforceable, which is a consumer-protection question.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: His contract caps his liability at the $450 fee — the fight is whether that consumer term is even enforceable.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an exculpatory consumer-contract clause is the central battle
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: There's no liability cap — he simply never inspected the crawlspace he certified, and the rot he missed costs $60,000.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No cap in the contract — he certified a crawlspace he never entered, and the rot is $60,000.
scenarios.vetting_note: Same missed defects; the cap clause shifts the case's center of gravity.
